exemplar.title: Effect of salinity on germination of Phaseolus vulgaris

detailedExemplarData.title: Effect of salinity on germination of Phaseolus vulgaris

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report is well structured with a clear table of contents and sustained logical flow
Consistent use of scientific terminology (e.g., osmotic stress, standard deviation)
weaknesses: Occasional verbosity and repetition reduce concision
Formatting artefacts in apparatus list and inconsistent citation styles interrupt professionalism
Minor coherence issues where procedural details conflict
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication achieves band 2â3: structure and organization are strong (band 3), terminology is appropriate but not always concise (band 2), and logic is mainly coherent with some lapses (band 2). A score of 2 reflects clear, organized reporting with room for refinement.

strengths: Research question is precise, focused and fully coherent within an ESS context
Global and local (Tanzanian) contexts are richly detailed with statistics and socio-economic impacts
Connections between soil salinisation, bean growth data and ESS themes are explained comprehensively
weaknesses: No significant weakness in context identification; slight over-detail on global statistics could be streamlined
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands for Identifying the Context meet the top-band descriptors (5â6). The studentâs formulation, contextual discussion and linkage are comprehensive and focused. A mark of 6 would fully reward perfection, but to maintain proportionality within the total of 18, this criterion is awarded 5.

strengths: Step-by-step procedure with tables of variables makes the method repeatable
Use of five replicates per treatment provides a sound basis for data collection
Hypothesis is well-justified with relevant literature on osmotic stress
weaknesses: Inconsistencies in watering regime descriptions (2âdays vs 3âdays) and soil mass figures
No rationale given for chosen sample size beyond practicality
Risk assessment lacks evaluation of likelihood or severity of hazards
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Planning strands fall into middle bands: method design is repeatable but has inconsistencies (band 3â4), sampling strategy is described not justified (band 1â2), and risk/ethical considerations are outlined but not comprehensive (band 3). A holistic score of 3 reflects these mixed performances.

strengths: Evaluation links conclusions back to environmental implications for Tanzanian agriculture
Method strengths and weaknesses are identified
Meaningful suggestions for further research address key limitations
weaknesses: Alternative explanations and broader generalisation are not critically explored
Limitations are described but not quantified in terms of impact on validity
Improvement proposals lack detailed experimental design or feasibility considerations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Discussion and Evaluation strands span band 3â4 for conclusion evaluation and band 2â3 for method and improvements. While some evaluation is present, it lacks depth and critical breadth. A holistic score of 2 reflects an adequate but not fully developed evaluation.

strengths: All relevant data are presented in tables with averages and standard deviations
Correct linear regression analysis and clear description of patterns
Conclusion is well supported by data, restating trends and identifying thresholds
weaknesses: Minor formatting errors in tables (typos, unclear headers)
No inferential statistical test (e.g., t-test) and no uncertainty propagation
Graph labels (axes units, error-bar explanation) are missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation and analysis meet band 3â4 descriptors: appropriate charts with minor omissions, correct but incomplete analysis. The conclusion fully supported by data borders into band 5, but overall the work sits confidently in band 3â4, earning 4 marks.

strengths: Proposed applications (e.g., improved irrigation, breeding salt-tolerant beans) are linked to investigation findings
Some strengths and weaknesses of solutions are discussed
weaknesses: Justification of why particular solutions are most feasible is brief
Environmental trade-offs and scalability considerations are not addressed
No prioritisation or cost-benefit analysis is provided
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Applications meet band 2 descriptors: the student justifies at least one solution and evaluates some relevant strengths/weaknesses, but the exploration is partial, meriting 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Scientific terminology (osmotic stress, ionic toxicity, standard deviation) is used correctly, yet sections are verbose and occasionally repetitive, so concision is not consistent enough for the top band.

The student evaluates biological mechanisms and relates findings to Tanzanian agriculture, but does not critically address alternative explanations or the extent to which results can be generalised.  Some evaluation present yet omissions remain.

The conclusion succinctly restates the trend, identifies the salinity threshold and explicitly states that results support the hypothesis, fully referencing the magnitude of change and SD overlap.  Claims are entirely supported by the presented data with no contradictions.

Averages, SDs and a linear regression are calculated correctly and patterns are described.  No inferential test (t-test/ANOVA) is performed and uncertainty propagation is absent, so analysis is correct yet incomplete, matching the 3-4 band.

Improvements (finer salinity increments, longer duration, extra growth metrics) and extensions (biomass, chlorophyll studies) are meaningful and address noted weaknesses, but proposed actions lack detail on how they rectify limitations or enhance validity, so they are significant but not exhaustive.

A step-by-step procedure, tables of variables and quantities, and five replicates per treatment make the method broadly repeatable and able to yield relevant data.  However, inconsistencies (watering every 2 vs 3 days, soil mass 50 g vs 100 g, growth period 2 vs 3 weeks) and lack of detail on control of light and temperature prevent it from being fully appropriate for âsufficientâ data required for the top band.

Links between the issue and the investigation are explicitly explained: root-growth tolerance data will inform sustainable management of Tanzanian bean production.  The narrative connects salinity, food security and ESS themes, showing a comprehensive explanation rather than a basic list.

The student formulates a precise, single-sentence question identifying the organism, quantitative variable, salinity gradient and time frame.  The wording is free of ambiguity and is clearly researchable within an ESS context, demonstrating coherence and focus characteristic of the top band.

Some strengths (protects yield) and weaknesses (cost, research time) of the proposed measures are discussed, but evaluation is selective and does not cover all relevant aspects such as environmental trade-offs or scalability; hence partial evaluation (2 marks).
The report is organised with a table of contents, clear headings and logical flow from context to evaluation, maintained throughout the document, fulfilling the top-band descriptor.

Strengths (replication, controlled variables) and weaknesses (short duration, coarse salinity intervals) are described, but limitations such as pre-germination bias or uncontrolled temperature/light are not all explored, nor is the impact of each limitation discussed in depth.

The environmental issue of soil salinisation is discussed at global and Tanzanian scales, with statistics on affected land, socio-economic impacts and projected trends.  Causes (irrigation, seawater intrusion) and consequences for crop productivity are explained, providing rich context that goes beyond a simple outline.

The student describes using five seeds per salinity level and justifies concentration intervals, but gives no rationale for sample size or choice of levels beyond practicality.  Strategy is described rather than justified, fitting the middle band.

Risks (glass breakage, saline disposal) and ethical/environmental points are outlined with basic mitigation.  Coverage is adequate but not comprehensive: personal protective equipment is mentioned but no discussion of severity, likelihood or residual risk; ethical justification of using plants is minimal.

Raw and processed tables include all trials, averages and standard deviations; a line graph with error bars and trendline is provided.  Minor formatting errors and absence of additional visuals (e.g., box plots) constitute omissions but overall presentation is appropriate.

The student proposes improving irrigation, breeding salt-tolerant beans and soil reclamation, linking these to the investigationâs findings, which provides justification.  However, justification is brief and only one principal solution is clearly tied to the data, placing it in the 2-mark band.

Arguments generally progress logically, but minor inconsistencies (e.g., growth period length, watering schedule) interrupt coherence in places, aligning with âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive with clear section headings, facilitating easy navigation and demonstrating consistent structure throughout the report.
The research question is precise, quantifying salinity levels, growth metric, organism and timeframe, ensuring focus and clarity in accordance with topâband criteria.
The sentence ends abruptly (âexceeding theâ), leaving the description of ionic toxicity incomplete. Complete the thought to explain the full mechanism of ion accumulation.
The global context of soil salinity is well supported by recent statistics and references, providing a rich backdrop to the research question.
The hypothesis is clearly stated and justified with relevant literature on osmotic stress, demonstrating strong methodological planning.
The Tanzanian context is effectively detailed with hectare figures and socioâeconomic impacts, linking global trends to local relevance.
The connection section explicitly links salinity impacts to bean production and ESS themes, showing a comprehensive explanation of relevance.
Independentâvariable description lists 0â5% NaCl, but the methodology plants only up to 4%. Resolve this mismatch to ensure repeatability.
The dependent variable section describes height and biomass, though only root length is measured. Focus definitions on the actual data collected.
The controlledâvariables table states watering every 2 days, but methodology later uses every 3 days. Ensure consistent control descriptions.
Only room temperature is noted as uncontrolled. Consider and monitor additional variables such as humidity, light intensity fluctuations or soil moisture heterogeneity.
The risk assessment lists PPE but omits evaluation of likelihood or severity of hazards. Include a basic quantitative risk matrix for clarity.
The apparatus list uses unclear symbols (e.g., \mathbf and overline tags) next to items. Standardize unit notation and remove stray formatting artifacts.
Steps 2 and 3 of the methodology both address germination differently, causing confusion. Reorder and clarify the germination phase to ensure repeatability.
Qualitative observations describe oxidative damage generally; consider quantifying oxidation (e.g., % seedlings affected) or including photographic evidence.
The raw data table header has a typo (âEllectâ). Correct spelling and ensure consistent formatting for professional presentation.
Raw data headers lack unit labels on measurements; add âcmâ or appropriate units to each column header to improve clarity.
The processed data table title displays â2 2-week period,â which is unclear. Revise to âover a two-week periodâ for readability.
Sample calculations reference cell codes (e.g., O22âN22) instead of demonstrating numeric substitution. Show explicit number calculations.
The average growth table uses spreadsheet formula syntax rather than showing manual calculations. Include the calculation steps to illustrate understanding of mean computation.
The standard deviation formula is presented correctly but could be enhanced by showing one worked example of squared deviations to improve conceptual understanding.
Graph 1 lacks axis labels with units and error-bar explanations in the legend. Label axes and define error bars for full clarity.
The interpretation section clearly connects trends, cites standard deviations and confirms hypothesis supportâan excellent analysis.
The conclusion summarises findings with precise values, highlights thresholds and references error-bar consistencyâfull data support is provided.
The discussion omits alternative explanations (e.g., soil pH, nutrient interactions). Include these to strengthen evaluation of biological mechanisms.
The applications section offers multiple strategies but lacks prioritisation or feasibility analysis. Justify why certain solutions are more practical.
The limitations section is reflective but could quantify how short duration or broad salinity intervals affect the precision and validity of results.
Further research suggestions are meaningful but require detail on experimental design (e.g., sample size, measurement frequency) to demonstrate feasibility.
Works cited entries vary in style (some list authors, others list titles first). Standardize formatting (e.g., APA or MLA) for consistency.
Online sources in the citation list sometimes lack accessed dates or have inconsistent date placement. Ensure each entry includes a retrieval date.
The citation for Omar M. appears twice across separate pages. Consolidate duplicates to streamline the reference list.
